topics.title: Records, Sealed Classes & Pattern Matching
topics.description: Java 17+ introduced three major productivity features: Records (immutable data carriers with auto-generated equals/hashCode/toString), Sealed Classes (restrict which classes can extend a type), and Pattern Matching (instanceof with binding variables, switch expressions with type patterns). Together they enable algebraic data types — the foundation of modern functional-style Java.
topics.story: Imagine a parcel service. Every parcel is one of: a Letter, a Box, or a Fragile item. Before sealed classes, anyone could create a new parcel type — Submarine, CoffeeMug — breaking all the switch statements. With sealed classes, the service director (compiler) knows exactly which parcel types exist. Records are like stamped parcel labels: immutable, automatically printed with all the fields. Pattern matching lets a sorter look at a parcel and say 'if it's a Box, open it and check width' — all in one line.
topics.analogy: Record = final class + constructor + equals + hashCode + toString in one line. Sealed = `permits` clause limits subclasses to known set. Pattern matching `instanceof` = cast + null check in one expression. Switch pattern = exhaustive dispatch over sealed hierarchy — compiler warns if a case is missing. Record components = auto-generated private final fields.
topics.memoryAnchor: record Point(int x, int y) {} — all-args constructor, accessors x() y(), equals, hashCode, toString auto-generated. sealed interface Shape permits Circle, Rect, Triangle. switch (shape) { case Circle c -> c.radius() } — exhaustive; compiler error if case missing. instanceof with binding: if (obj instanceof String s) { use s }. Records can implement interfaces. Records cannot extend classes (they extend Record implicitly). Compact canonical constructor: record Range(int lo, int hi) { Range { if (lo > hi) throw new … } }
topics.firstPrinciples: Records: a class that is transparently a data holder. All fields are final. The compiler generates: a canonical constructor, accessor methods named after components (not getX()), equals() comparing all components, hashCode() over all components, toString() listing all components. Sealed classes: the `sealed` keyword + `permits` clause tells the compiler the complete set of direct subtypes. Each permitted subtype must be `final`, `sealed`, or `non-sealed`. Enables exhaustive switch without a default. Pattern matching instanceof: `if (obj instanceof String s)` — checks type AND binds the variable `s` in the true branch. Eliminates the explicit cast. Switch patterns (Java 21 GA): `switch(obj) { case String s -> ...; case Integer i -> ...; }` — dispatches on type.
topics.bruteForce: Before records: public final class Point { private final int x; private final int y; public Point(int x, int y) { this.x=x; this.y=y; } public int getX(){return x;} public int getY(){return y;} @Override public boolean equals(...){...} @Override public int hashCode(){...} @Override public String toString(){...} } — ~30 lines for a simple value type.
topics.optimizedApproach: record Point(int x, int y) {} — 1 line. Sealed + records create type-safe, exhaustive domain models. Pattern matching switch replaces if-instanceof-cast chains. Lombok becomes largely unnecessary for data classes.
topics.whenToUse: Records: DTO/value objects, response payloads, configuration objects, local intermediate results. Sealed: domain models with finite variants (Result<T>, Shape, Command types), API responses. Pattern matching: replacing instanceof-cast chains, visitor pattern replacement, JSON/union type dispatch. NOT: records for mutable entities (use JPA entity instead — JPA needs no-arg constructor and setters).

examples.title: Records + Sealed Classes + Pattern Matching
examples.explanation: Records auto-generate constructor, accessors (x(), y() not getX()), equals, hashCode, toString. Sealed interface Shape with permits Circle, Rect, Triangle. The switch on Shape is exhaustive — adding a new permitted type causes a compile error if not handled. Pattern matching instanceof eliminates explicit casts. Compact canonical constructor runs validation inside the record.
examples.realWorldUse: Spring Boot REST response: record ApiResponse<T>(boolean success, T data, String error) {}. Domain commands: sealed interface Command permits CreateOrder, CancelOrder, UpdateStatus {}. Each command is a record. Command handler uses switch pattern — exhaustive, no default needed.

problems.title: Shape Area Calculator with Records
problems.description:
Given a list of shapes described as strings in format `CIRCLE:r`, `RECT:w:h`, or `TRIANGLE:base:height`, compute the total area.

Circle area = π * r². Rect area = w * h. Triangle area = 0.5 * base * height.

Input: each line is a shape string
Output: total area rounded to 2 decimal places

Sample:
```
CIRCLE:5
RECT:4:3
TRIANGLE:6:4
```
Expected: `103.54`
problems.hint1: Define sealed interface Shape with record implementations for Circle, Rect, Triangle.
problems.hint2: Parse each line by splitting on ':'. Use a factory method to create the right Shape subtype.
problems.hint3: Use switch pattern `switch(shape) { case Circle c -> ...; case Rect r -> ...; case Triangle t -> ...; }` to compute area without default.
problems.editorial: Sealed interface Shape with three record implementations. Switch pattern on Shape is exhaustive — compiler guarantees all cases handled. Records expose components via accessor methods (r(), w(), h(), etc.). No instanceof casts needed. Math.PI * r * r for circle, direct multiplication for rect and triangle.

problems.title: Validate Records with Compact Constructor
problems.description:
Create a `record DateRange(int startYear, int endYear)` that validates `startYear <= endYear` in its compact constructor, throwing `IllegalArgumentException` with message `"startYear > endYear"` if invalid.

Read two integers per line (startYear endYear). For valid ranges print `"OK: startYear-endYear"`. For invalid print `"ERR: <message>"`.

Sample input:
```
2020 2025
2025 2020
2023 2023
```
Expected:
```
OK: 2020-2025
ERR: startYear > endYear
OK: 2023-2023
```
problems.hint1: Use a compact canonical constructor: record DateRange(int startYear, int endYear) { DateRange { /* validation */ } }
problems.hint2: Wrap `new DateRange(...)` in a try-catch to handle the IllegalArgumentException.
problems.hint3: In the catch block, print `"ERR: " + e.getMessage()`.
problems.editorial: Compact canonical constructor runs after the auto-generated assignments but before the object is returned. Use it for validation — the component parameters (startYear, endYear) are already assigned when the compact constructor body runs. Accessor methods are named after components (startYear(), not getStartYear()).

problems.title: JSON-Like Node with Pattern Matching
problems.description:
Implement a simplified JSON value type using sealed interface + records, then evaluate an expression tree.

Nodes: `JsonNull`, `JsonBool(boolean v)`, `JsonNum(double v)`, `JsonStr(String v)`.

Read lines of form `TYPE:value` and print their string representation:
- `NULL` → `null`
- `BOOL:true` → `true`
- `NUM:42.5` → `42.5`
- `STR:hello` → `"hello"`

Sample:
```
NULL
BOOL:false
NUM:3.14
STR:world
```
Expected:
```
null
false
3.14
"world"
```
problems.hint1: Define `sealed interface JsonNode permits JsonNull, JsonBool, JsonNum, JsonStr {}` with record implementations.
problems.hint2: JsonNull has no components. Use a singleton or just `new JsonNull()` (records with no components are valid).
problems.hint3: Use switch pattern to dispatch: `case JsonNull n -> "null"; case JsonBool b -> String.valueOf(b.v()); case JsonNum n -> ...`
problems.editorial: Sealed interface with four record subtypes models JSON value types — a classic algebraic data type. Switch pattern is exhaustive: compiler verifies all four permitted types are handled. Records with no components (JsonNull) are valid. The yield keyword returns a value from a block case in a switch expression.
